file_name: Raghavendra et al. - 2023 - Deep learning–based skin lesion multi-class classification with global average pooling improvement.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) model with global average pooling for multi-class classification of skin lesions. The model was trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset to classify seven classes of skin cancer/lesions, achieving 97.20% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and training of a novel deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) model with global average pooling for multi-class skin lesion classification. It reports specific results (97.20% accuracy) on the HAM10000 dataset, compares the proposed model against existing transfer learning models (ResNet50, VGG-16, etc.), and includes a methodology section detailing preprocessing and model architecture, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Deep Learning–Based Skin Lesion Multi‑class Classification with Global Average Pooling Improvement
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) model incorporating global average pooling for the multi-class classification of skin lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven classes of skin cancer and benign lesions. Preprocessing steps included black hat filtering for artifact removal and data augmentation to balance the dataset. The proposed model's performance was compared against state-of-the-art transfer learning models, including ResNet50, VGG-16, MobileNetV2, and DenseNet121. The proposed DCNN achieved a testing accuracy of 97.20%, along with high precision, recall, and F1 scores of 0.97, outperforming the compared models. The results demonstrate that the proposed model is more efficient, reliable, and computationally less complex than existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class classification. Classified under squamous cell carcinoma category.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for seven specific skin cancer and benign lesion types. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (97.20%), precision, recall, F1 score, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the Harvard Dataverse repository consisting of 10,015 skin lesion images with high diversity among classes, acquired from different modalities and populations. It includes metadata such as lesion_id, dx, image_id, sex, dx_type, localization, and age.

use.training: Used for training the proposed DCNN model after data augmentation and balancing.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed model and comparing it with transfer learning models (ResNet50, VGG-16, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and baseline models were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.preprocessing: Black hat filtering and data augmentation (training images increased to 45,756)
HAM10000.notes: The proposed DCNN model achieved the highest accuracy (97.20%) among the compared models. It also achieved the highest average AUC-ROC score (0.9969). The model was trained for 50 epochs with a batch size of 64. The proposed model outperformed VGG16, MobileNetV2, ResNet50, and DenseNet121 in terms of overall accuracy and class-wise performance, particularly for the 'nv', 'mel', and 'bkl' classes.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (Proposed model was trained from scratch; transfer learning models VGG16, MobileNetV2, ResNet50, and DenseNet121 were used for comparison)

architecture_details.inspiration: VGG16 architecture and basic CNN architecture
architecture_details.key_innovation: Global Average Pooling instead of flattening before the dense layer

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5-8250U CPU, 8 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce MX130 GPU

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed DCNN (Deep Convolutional Neural Network with Global Average Pooling)
preprocessing_techniques: CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) for color and visual contrast enhancement
Conventional contrast enhancement using PIL ImageEnhance module with a factor of 0.5
Image sharpening using PIL Image filter
Black hat filtering (morphological transformation) for removal of black hair artifacts
Data augmentation (resampling) to balance class distribution, including horizontal/vertical flipping, width/height shifting (20%), and rotation (10 degrees)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with True setting for mirror reflection.
Applied with True setting for mirror reflection.
Shifted in the horizontal direction (X-axis) by 20% (range 0.2).
Shifted in the vertical direction (Y-axis) by 20% (range 0.2).
Generated images with rotation from -10 to 10 degrees (range 10).
Stretched the image angle slantly by a factor of 0.2 (range 0.2).
Used 'nearest' to fill spaces left over after shifting with the closest pixel value.
Used to balance class distribution across all classes in the HAM10000 dataset, increasing training images to 45,756. Not performed on the test dataset.

conclusions: The proposed novel deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) model incorporating global average pooling achieved a testing accuracy of 97.20%, which is the highest among the compared state-of-the-art models for multi-class skin lesion classification.

The proposed DCNN model outperformed transfer learning models including ResNet50, VGG-16, MobileNetV2, and DenseNet121 in terms of overall accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and average AUC-ROC (0.9969).

The integration of black hat filtering for artifact removal and data augmentation (resampling) significantly improved the performance of the proposed DCNN model by balancing the dataset and reducing noise.
The proposed model is computationally less complex and consumes less memory compared to existing deep learning models, making it more efficient for clinical deployment.

The efficacy of the proposed model was validated through a graphical user interface (GUI), demonstrating its potential as a dependable computer-aided dermatologist tool for accurate identification and classification of skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 'nv' class comprises 67% of samples) and potential data leakage issues due to multiple images from the same patients; the paper does not report patient-level splitting to mitigate this risk.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or clinical settings.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons between the proposed DCNN and baseline models (ResNet50, VGG-16, etc.), relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The proposed model was trained from scratch without pre-training, which contrasts with the transfer learning approach used for the baseline models, potentially confounding the comparison of architectural efficiency versus pre-training benefits.

The hardware used for training (Intel Core i5-8250U CPU, 8 GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce MX130 GPU) is relatively low-end, which may affect the reproducibility of training times and computational complexity claims in standard high-performance environments.

The study claims computational efficiency with only 108,471 trainable parameters compared to millions in baseline models, but this simplicity may limit the model's capacity to capture complex features compared to deeper architectures, a trade-off not deeply analyzed.
The GUI validation is presented as proof of efficacy but lacks quantitative user study or clinical workflow integration metrics, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical utility.

dataset_usage_details.source_pdf_path: /home/agarciag/Desktop/skin_cancer_review/llm_agent/pdfs/compliant/processed_Raghavendra et al. - 2023 - Deep learning–based skin lesion multi-class classification with global average pooling improvement.pdf
dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (also referred to as ISIC 2018 in some contexts within the paper's metadata, but explicitly named HAM10000 in the text) for both training and testing. The dataset contains 10,015 images across seven classes. The data was split into 80% for training (8,012 images) and 20% for testing (2,003 images). Data augmentation (resampling) was applied to the training set to balance class distribution, increasing the training images to 45,756. No external validation or cross-dataset testing was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset using a fixed internal split.

training.notes: Used for model fitting. Resampling was applied to balance classes. Black hat filtering and other preprocessing were applied.

validation.counts: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out validation set; validation metrics likely derived from training loop callbacks or the training split itself
validation.notes: The paper reports 'Validation accuracy' and 'Validation loss' during training (e.g., 98.03% accuracy, 0.0857 loss for proposed model), but does not specify if this refers to a separate validation split or the training set performance during epochs. Early stopping and learning rate reduction were used as callbacks.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. No augmentation was applied to this set.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Baseline models (ResNet50, VGG-16, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121) are transfer learning models, implying pretraining on ImageNet. The proposed DCNN was trained from scratch.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques including horizontal/vertical flipping, width/height shifting (20%), rotation (10 degrees), and shear were applied. Resampling was used to balance class distribution, increasing training samples from 8,012 to 45,756.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 80% training (8,012 images), 20% testing (2,003 images). Patient-level separation is not reported; image-level split is implied.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set is a held-out portion of the HAM10000 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Highest accuracy among compared models. Class-wise metrics provided in Table 8.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or clinical settings.
The paper does not report patient-level splitting, raising potential data leakage concerns due to multiple images from the same patients in the dataset.
Class imbalance in the original dataset (nv class is 67%) was addressed via augmentation/resampling on the training set only.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images in the validation set (if distinct from training).
Patient-level split details (whether images from the same patient were in both train and test sets).
Specific hyperparameters for baseline models other than architecture.
Statistical significance testing results (p-values) for performance comparisons.
